title: Beef goulash
subtitle: Hungarian beef stew, thick with sweet paprika and onion.
excerpt: Beef shin braised low and slow with a great deal of sweet paprika, onion, caraway and green pepper until the sauce is thick, richly coloured and the meat is falling apart. Hungary's national stew. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Hungarian beef goulash (gulyás). The dish originates with Hungarian cattle herders and first appeared as a national dish in cookbooks in the 19th century. August Escoffier documented a version in Le Guide Culinaire (1903, public domain). The dish is central to Hungarian national identity; the Austro-Hungarian Empire spread versions of it across Central Europe.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to three months. Defrost overnight; reheat gently on the hob.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Better the next day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before. Reheat on the hob over low heat.

content.text: Goulash uses a remarkable quantity of paprika — far more than feels instinctive. Three tablespoons of sweet paprika is the correct amount for four people, not a half teaspoon. The paprika is the dish. It must go into hot fat and be stirred for 30 seconds before the liquid is added, to bloom the flavour; it must not be skimped.

Hungarian sweet paprika is different from smoked paprika. Use proper Hungarian paprika if possible; it is milder and sweeter than other varieties. The combination of sweet and smoked paprika here approximates the flavour.
Fry the onion and bloom the paprika

Heat {{lard}} of lard in a large casserole over medium heat. Cook {{onion}} diced onion for 12 minutes until very soft and beginning to caramelise. Add {{garlic}} of chopped garlic and {{pepper-green}} diced green pepper; cook 3 minutes. Remove from the heat. Stir in {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika, {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika and {{caraway-seeds}} of caraway seeds. Return to low heat and stir for 30 seconds.

Add {{beef-shin}} of beef cubes, {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée, {{stock-beef}} of stock, {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook on the lowest possible heat for 1.5 to 2 hours until the beef is very tender and the sauce is thick and coating. Remove the bay leaves. Serve with soured cream.

items.cause: Not enough paprika, or liquid added before paprika bloomed.
items.fix: This dish needs 3 tablespoons of paprika; it is not a mistake. Bloom the paprika in hot fat before adding any liquid.

items.cause: Paprika burned in the fat before liquid was added.
items.fix: Remove the pan from the heat before adding paprika. It blooms from residual heat; 30 seconds off the heat is safer than 30 seconds on.

items.cause: Pieces too large, or simmering too vigorously.
items.fix: Cut beef to 4 cm maximum. Keep the heat at the lowest setting; a vigorous boil makes the beef tough.

content.text: Goulash is the national dish of Hungary, rooted in the cattle-herding tradition of the Great Plain and carried across Central Europe by the Austro-Hungarian Empire. Every country with a connection to that history has a version. The Hungarian original is the simplest and most austere: beef, onion and paprika, nothing more. Serve with egg noodles, soured cream and a cold beer.
